narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50—above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on past students' data to predict bar exam success on the first try. The model assessed your profile, including your Year 1 decile rank of 2, Year 3 decile of 1, middle family income bracket, LSAT score of 30, and full-time student status, and it estimated a 40.3% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This elevated risk threshold led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to pass with high confidence. Among all the factors considered, your undergraduate GPA stood out as the strongest positive influence, pushing the prediction toward bar exam success because it signals strong academic preparation compared to most applicants. However, your Year 3 decile rank of 1—well below the average of 5.53—emerged as the second most powerful factor, significantly pulling the prediction toward failure by highlighting a notable dip in school-relative performance later in your studies. Your LSAT score of 30, lower than the typical 36.70, had a milder but still notable negative effect, nudging the odds slightly toward failure in the model's eyes. Overall, while your GPA helped balance things out, the combination of weaker later-year grades and a below-average LSAT outweighed it, placing your predicted failure risk higher than what the school deems acceptable for most admitted students. In contrast to typical applicants who pass, you ranked lower in key performance deciles despite your stronger undergrad record, which the model weighed heavily. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, and reflect on what may have impacted your Year 3 performance to strengthen future applications. Strengthening these areas could lower your predicted failure risk below 40% in a re-evaluation. You might also seek feedback from advisors on bolstering your later academic deciles through additional coursework or explanations in your personal statement. With focused effort, a stronger profile awaits in your next application cycle.
